screening_questions.question: When a big project at work or church falls behind schedule and things feel chaotic, I naturally...

options.text: Start mapping out a clear plan and assigning responsibilities
Rally the team, remind them why it matters, and get everyone moving again
Roll up my sleeves and do whatever needs to be done, no task is beneath me

screening_questions.question: When someone in my friend group is going through something really hard, I naturally...

options.text: Show up and sit with them, even when there is nothing to fix
Start thinking about who else can support them and how to coordinate that over the long haul
Text them practical things I can do, like bring dinner or watch their kids

screening_questions.question: When I meet someone who does not share my faith, I naturally...

options.text: Feel drawn to share my story and see if it sparks curiosity
Want to make sure they feel genuinely welcomed and comfortable around me
Trust that God has a plan for them and pray for the right moment

screening_questions.question: When I have extra money at the end of the month, I naturally...

options.text: Think about who or what could really use it right now
Look for ways to be strategic and put it toward something with lasting impact
Think about where things are heading and invest it toward that future

screening_questions.question: When I am listening to a message or reading about faith, I often notice...

options.text: Gaps, inconsistencies, or things that were not fully explained, and I want to dig in
Stories of transformation that I want to pass on to people I know
Principles I can apply to how I organize or lead what I am already doing

screening_questions.question: When I think about my role in a group or community, I most naturally gravitate toward...

options.text: Setting the vision and helping people see where things could go
Making sure everyone feels seen and included, especially new people
Coming alongside the people who are struggling or on the edges

screening_questions.question: When someone pushes back on something I believe strongly, I naturally...

options.text: Calmly explain my reasoning and invite them to think it through with me
Hold my position without anxiety, trusting that truth will eventually land
Try to understand their point of view and feel what they are feeling before I respond

screening_questions.question: When I walk into a room where I do not know anyone, I naturally...

options.text: Spot the person standing alone and go introduce myself
Start observing how the group functions and whether it is organized well
Look for someone who seems discouraged and try to lift their spirits

screening_questions.question: When someone on my team is struggling with their role, I naturally...

options.text: Find out what they need and help fill those gaps myself so they are not stuck
Sit with them, ask what is really going on, and help them figure out next steps
Tell them honestly what I see in them and why I believe they can do this

screening_questions.question: When I see a need that nobody else seems to be addressing, I naturally...

options.text: Step up and fill it myself, even if no one notices
Organize a response and recruit others to help make it sustainable
Give generously toward meeting that need even if I can only do a little

screening_questions.question: I feel most alive when I am...

options.text: Explaining something complex in a way that finally clicks for someone
Helping someone push through a moment of doubt and come out stronger
Leading a group of people toward a shared goal that really matters

screening_questions.question: I feel most alive when I am...

options.text: Welcoming someone new and watching them go from awkward to at home
Doing something practical that frees someone else up to do what they do best
Sitting with someone in a really hard moment, not trying to fix it, just being there

screening_questions.question: I feel most alive when I am...

options.text: Sharing something about my own faith journey with someone who is genuinely curious
Giving in a way that I can see the direct impact it makes on someone's life
Trusting God in a situation where there is absolutely no obvious way forward

screening_questions.question: I feel most alive when I am...

options.text: Walking with someone through a long, hard season and watching them come out different
Setting up the systems and structures that make everything else possible
Sitting down with someone who has real questions about faith and life

screening_questions.question: I feel most alive when I am...

options.text: Watching someone I believed in take a big step they were too afraid to take before
Watching a community I helped build really click together and feel like home
Seeing someone far from God take a real step toward faith

screening_questions.question: I feel most alive when I am...

options.text: Believing God for something that seems impossible, and staying steady while others panic
Catching the behind-the-scenes details that everyone else missed before they became problems
Pouring resources into something I believe is going to change lives

screening_questions.question: I feel most alive when I am...

options.text: Taking a long walk with someone who is carrying something too heavy to carry alone
Casting a vision that gets people genuinely excited about the future
Being the kind of steady presence in someone's life that helps them grow over years

screening_questions.question: People most often come to me for...

options.text: Help making sense of something confusing or complex
A word of encouragement when they feel like giving up
Someone to just listen without trying to fix anything

screening_questions.question: People most often come to me for...

options.text: Help organizing something they cannot figure out how to pull off
A safe place to land when they are new or feeling out of place
Practical help, like moving, fixing something, or just getting something done

screening_questions.question: People most often come to me for...

options.text: Direction on a big decision or help seeing the bigger picture
Advice on how to give generously or use resources in a way that matters
Someone to pray with when they are scared and do not know what to do

screening_questions.question: People most often come to me for...

options.text: Help navigating a hard season in their personal or spiritual life over the long term
Someone to help them process hard questions about God and life without judgment
A fresh perspective when they have lost hope and need someone to believe for them

screening_questions.question: People most often come to me for...

options.text: Practical logistics help, like planning an event or coordinating a group
Someone to hype them up and remind them why they can do this
Help thinking through how to share their faith with someone specific

screening_questions.question: People most often come to me for...

options.text: A warm meal, a spare room, or a place where they feel genuinely welcome
Someone to pray with them and believe alongside them for something they are trusting God for
Someone who will show up and help without being asked

screening_questions.question: People most often come to me for...

options.text: Help figuring out how to give wisely and make a real impact with their resources
Honest truth spoken with care, not just what they want to hear
Someone to listen without judgment when they are carrying something really painful

screening_questions.question: When I see a task that needs to be done, I just do it. I do not wait to be asked.
I find it easy to believe God will come through, even when everything around me looks bad.
I genuinely enjoy having strangers or new people in my home or personal space.
I love breaking down big ideas into smaller, clearer pieces until the other person truly gets it.
I feel a strong pull to stay close to people who are hurting, even when their pain is uncomfortable or complicated.
When I hear about someone who does not know God, I feel a genuine urgency, not just a passing thought.
I naturally think about systems, structures, and how to make things run more smoothly.
I get a deep sense of satisfaction from giving money or resources to something that matters, even if no one knows I did it.
When someone feels stuck or defeated, I usually find something specific to say that helps them get moving again.
I naturally think in terms of where things are heading and I feel responsible for helping people get there.
I check on people weeks or months after a crisis, not just in the moment. Long-term follow-through comes naturally to me.
I have given away more than I probably should have by most people's standards, and I do not regret it.
People in pain draw me in rather than overwhelm me. I want to be closer, not farther away.
I would rather serve behind the scenes and see the thing succeed than be up front getting credit.
I tend to know what is going on in the lives of the people around me and I make sure no one gets lost in the shuffle.
When I introduce myself to someone, I am genuinely curious about their story, and I remember what they tell me.

level_1.question: When I join a new team or project, I usually spot the missing systems or gaps within the first week.
I keep detailed lists, color-coded notes, or step-by-step plans for things other people handle casually.
When a process is broken or inefficient, I do not just notice it, I propose a fix and follow through.
I naturally take on the role of tracking who is doing what and by when, even when no one asked me to.
When a big event needs to be planned from scratch, I naturally gravitate toward...

options.text: Creating the master checklist and making sure every detail is assigned to someone
Getting people excited about it and making sure it builds community
Figuring out the finances and making sure resources are used wisely

level_2.question: The thing that frustrates me most is when people jump into execution before there is a clear plan.
I feel most satisfied when I turn something chaotic into something that actually runs smoothly.
When a project succeeds because of the systems I put in place behind the scenes, that is enough for me.
I feel genuinely energized by solving logistical puzzles, like how to get 20 people, 10 tasks, and one deadline to line up.
When a project is behind schedule and morale is sinking, what energizes me most is...

options.text: Getting back to the plan, adjusting it, and re-clarifying who is doing what
Rallying people emotionally and reminding them why this matters
Identifying what is most broken and rolling up my sleeves to fix it

level_3.question: I believe that loving people well often looks like creating systems that do not let them fall through the cracks.
I think the unglamorous work of keeping things organized is just as sacred as anything that happens on stage.
I believe that chaos does not honor God, and that order creates space for people to thrive.
When I think about the legacy I want to leave, it includes the systems and structures that will keep working after I am gone.
I believe that a well-organized team can accomplish things that a talented but disorganized one never will.

level_1.question: When I learn something new and meaningful, my first instinct is to find someone to share it with.
I notice when an explanation is not quite right, and I feel compelled to re-explain it more clearly.
I keep going with an explanation until I can tell the other person actually understands it, not just nodded politely.
I often prepare more than necessary before leading a discussion, because I want to get it right.
When someone is confused about something I know well, I...

options.text: Walk them through it step by step until it genuinely clicks
Connect them to someone else who can explain it in a different way
Encourage them to trust the process and keep going even when it is hard

level_2.question: Watching someone's eyes light up when they finally understand something gives me a deep sense of purpose.
I am frustrated when good ideas are communicated poorly, because the idea deserves better.
I feel most energized when I am diving deep into a topic, not just skimming the surface.
I care about accuracy, not just warmth. I would rather tell someone a hard truth clearly than a comfortable half-truth kindly.
I feel most alive when I am...

options.text: Doing a deep dive into a topic I care about and figuring out how to explain it to others
Watching something I said or shared actually change the way someone acts or decides
Finding the simplest possible way to say something that everyone else makes complicated

level_3.question: I believe that clarity is a form of love, because confusion keeps people stuck.
I think most people are capable of understanding complex things if someone will just take the time to explain it well.
I believe that getting the details right matters, not just getting the big picture right.
I believe that a well-understood truth changes lives more than a hundred emotional experiences without any real foundation.
I think the church often moves too fast and does not slow down enough to help people truly understand what they believe and why.

level_1.question: When someone seems defeated, I almost always find something specific and true to say that shifts their perspective.
I notice when someone goes quiet in a group and I make a point to check in with them later.
I have been told more than once that a text or word I sent came at exactly the right moment, and I do not think that is a coincidence.
I keep track of the people around me who are working hard and not getting the recognition they deserve.
When a friend tells me they are thinking of quitting something important to them, I...

options.text: Tell them specifically what I have seen in them that makes me believe they can finish
Ask questions to understand what is really going on before I say anything
Help them think through a practical plan to make it more manageable

level_2.question: I feel something close to urgency when I see someone about to give up on something I believe they are capable of.
I am genuinely motivated by watching people take steps they were too afraid to take before I came alongside them.
It bothers me when people in a group are contributing a lot but never being acknowledged for it.
When I encourage someone, I am not just trying to make them feel better. I want them to see something true that they have been missing.
People most often come to me when...

options.text: They are about to do something scary and need someone firmly in their corner
They need help thinking through a decision step by step
They are in pain and need someone to sit with them without an agenda

level_3.question: I believe most people give up right before their breakthrough, and that one well-timed word can change that.
I think the world has enough critics. What it is short on is people who genuinely believe in others.
I believe that encouragement is not just nice, it is necessary. Without it, people shrink.
I think calling out what is genuinely good in a person is one of the most powerful things anyone can do.
I believe that believing in someone before they believe in themselves is one of the greatest gifts you can give another person.

level_1.question: When I hear about a real financial need, I almost immediately start thinking about what I can give toward it.
I give more than I tell people I give, and I actively prefer it that way.
I often think about money in terms of what it can do for others, not just what it can do for me.
I look for overlooked giving opportunities, like the person no one else is funding or the cause that is not trendy.
When I receive an unexpected financial windfall, my first move is usually to...

options.text: Think about who or what it could serve before I decide what to do with it
Think through how to steward it wisely and make it go as far as possible
Celebrate and share the joy with the people around me

level_2.question: I experience real joy when I give, not obligation. Generosity feels like freedom, not duty.
I feel bothered when I see resources hoarded or mismanaged by people who have been trusted with them.
When I give anonymously and someone is blessed without knowing it was me, that actually feels the best.
I think about giving even when finances are tight. It is not something I only do when I have extra.
I feel most alive when I am...

options.text: Being generous at a level that requires real trust that God will provide
Discovering a specific, concrete need I can meet immediately
Getting other people excited to give together toward something that matters

level_3.question: I genuinely believe that everything I have belongs to God first, and I am the steward, not the owner.
I think generosity is the antidote to fear, and I have seen that be true in my own life.
I believe money is neither good nor bad. It is a tool, and I feel a real sense of responsibility for how I use it.
I believe a community that gives freely builds a culture that can withstand almost anything.
I do not see tithing as a ceiling. It is more like the floor.

level_1.question: When a group I am part of lacks direction, I naturally start pointing people toward one, even without a formal role.
I am often the one who articulates where things are going when no one else has said it out loud.
I find myself thinking about how a decision made today will shape where things are in three to five years.
People tend to follow my lead even when I have not been given a title or formal authority.
When I am part of a team with no clear leader, I usually...

options.text: Start naturally organizing next steps and getting people aligned around a direction
Focus on what I can do with my own hands to move things forward
Make sure everyone in the room feels heard before any decisions get made

level_2.question: I feel most energized when I am casting a vision and watching people actually catch it.
When I see an organization or team underperforming because of unclear direction, I feel a pull to do something about it.
I do not lead for the recognition. I lead because I can see what is possible and it bothers me when it is not happening.
I find it more satisfying to lead someone else to success than to succeed on my own.
People most often come to me for...

options.text: Help seeing the bigger picture when they feel lost in the details
A practical plan to tackle something they have been putting off
Someone to pray with when they do not know which direction to go

level_3.question: I believe that good leadership is ultimately about releasing others, not holding power.
I think every group needs someone who sees further than the horizon and keeps pointing people in that direction.
I believe a leader's most important job is to build other leaders, not to build followers.
I think influence without character is dangerous, and I hold myself to a high standard because of it.
I believe the best vision is not just what I dream up alone. It is what God puts in my heart for people who genuinely need it.

level_1.question: When I hear about someone going through something painful, I want to be near them, not keep a safe distance.
I often sit with people in their pain long after others have moved on to fixing things or offering advice.
I notice when someone is suffering and trying to hide it, and I find a way to gently open a door for them.
When I am in a group and someone is clearly hurting, I cannot fully focus on anything else until I have checked on them.
When a friend is grieving a major loss, I...

options.text: Go to them, stay as long as they need, and let them lead the conversation
Coordinate a meal train and practical support with the people who care about them
Send a heartfelt note and make sure they know I am praying for them consistently

level_2.question: I find that being present with someone in pain, without saying or fixing anything, feels natural rather than uncomfortable.
I feel something almost physical when I hear about injustice or unnecessary suffering. It bothers me at a gut level.
What energizes me is not solving problems. It is making people feel less alone in them.
I get frustrated when people rush past real pain with quick answers, Bible verses, or silver linings before the person feels heard.
I feel most alive when I am...

options.text: Sitting in the middle of someone's mess with them and knowing it matters
Connecting someone to resources or support that actually make their situation better
Praying over someone who has lost hope and watching them feel something shift

level_3.question: I believe that presence is more powerful than solutions. Most people need to be felt with before they need to be fixed.
I think the church is too quick to celebrate healings and too slow to honor the people who quietly show up for the sick and the broken.
I believe compassion is not a feeling, it is a discipline. You choose to move toward people in pain even when it costs you.
I believe every person who is suffering deserves someone in their corner who is not trying to fix them or hurry them along.
I think empathy is not enough on its own. You have to actually show up and stay.

level_1.question: I get antsy at an event or gathering if there are chairs to set up, tasks to do, or messes to clean and I am just standing around.
I am the person who shows up early to help set up and stays late to help clean up, without being asked.
I often notice things that need to be done that other people walk right past.
I prefer to let my actions speak. I do not need to announce what I did or why.
When someone I care about moves to a new place, I...

options.text: Show up with boxes, my truck, and a full day of my time without thinking twice
Organize a group of people to help and coordinate who is doing what
Make sure they feel welcome in their new neighborhood and throw them a housewarming

level_2.question: I feel most useful when I am doing something concrete that directly makes someone's life easier.
I get frustrated when I see a practical need going unmet while people talk about it instead of just addressing it.
Knowing that something ran smoothly because of work I did in the background is deeply satisfying to me.
I do not need recognition to keep going. In fact, attention on what I did feels a little uncomfortable.
People most often come to me when...

options.text: Something practical needs to happen and they need someone who will actually do it
They need to vent to someone who will not judge them or try to fix them right away
They need someone to believe in them before a big moment

level_3.question: I believe that serving others is not a lesser calling. It is as holy as preaching or leading.
I believe no task is beneath me if it serves someone I love or a cause I believe in.
I think the most important people in any church are the ones no one ever sees, and I am proud to be one of them.
I believe that love is something you do, not something you say. Showing up is how I say it.
I think the world would change faster if more people stopped waiting to be asked and just started helping.

level_1.question: I bring up my faith naturally in regular conversations, not in a forced or preachy way.
When I meet someone who is searching for something but does not know what, I feel a natural pull to start a conversation.
I have had real conversations with people about faith in settings most Christians would never try, like a bar, a gym, or a waiting room.
I am not afraid of faith conversations with skeptics. I actually find them interesting, not threatening.
When I find out a coworker is going through something really hard and says they do not believe in God, I...

options.text: Look for an opening to share what my own faith has meant in hard seasons
Focus on being a reliable, caring presence without forcing anything
Invite them over for dinner and just get to know them better

level_2.question: Hearing that someone took a step toward faith because of a conversation I had with them is the most satisfying thing I can experience.
I feel a real urgency about people who do not know God, not guilt or pressure. More like a deep care for what they are missing.
I am not satisfied just maintaining community with the people who already believe. I want to keep reaching further out.
I get a little restless in communities where everyone is already a Christian and no one is thinking about the people just outside the door.
I feel most alive when I am...

options.text: In a real, genuine conversation with someone exploring faith for the first time
Training and equipping others to share their own stories more confidently
Gathering people together in a space that makes it easy for seekers to feel welcome

level_3.question: I believe every person deserves the chance to hear something real about faith from someone who actually cares about them.
I think most people are not anti-God. They are just waiting for someone to be honest and real with them about it.
I believe that if I have found something that changed my life, keeping it to myself is not humility, it is selfishness.
I think the church loses something critical when it gets so comfortable with insiders that outsiders cannot find a way in.
I believe that sharing faith is not about having all the answers. It is about being willing to walk with someone toward them.

level_1.question: I naturally keep mental track of where each person in my circle is in life, and when I have not heard from someone in a while, I reach out.
I am more energized by a two-hour conversation with one person going through something hard than by a large group gathering.
I follow up after hard conversations. Weeks later, I still remember and I circle back to check in.
I think in terms of seasons and long arcs for people. I am not just trying to help someone feel better today. I want to see them grow over years.
When I notice someone drifting away from a group or community, I...

options.text: Personally reach out and quietly try to find out what is really going on
Mention it to a leader and suggest a follow-up system so no one gets lost
Send them a message letting them know they are missed and believed in

level_2.question: Walking with someone through a difficult season over months or years gives me a sense of purpose that short-term interactions rarely do.
I feel protective of the people in my care. When one of them is being pressured, misled, or hurt, I will speak up.
I am bothered when communities celebrate big moments but do not have real systems for caring for people in the quiet, hard stretches in between.
I get real satisfaction not just from helping people in a crisis, but from watching them grow and become more whole over time.
People most often come to me when...

options.text: They are in a long, hard season and need someone who will not give up on them or rush the process
They have a specific emotional pain that needs someone to sit in it with them right now
They want a mentor who will be direct and help them grow, not just comfort them

level_3.question: I believe that caring for people's souls is a long game, not a sprint, and I am in it for the long haul.
I think every person needs at least one person who knows their whole story and will not leave when things get complicated.
I believe that real community is not built at big events. It is built in the quiet, consistent investment in individual people.
I think the measure of a healthy community is not how exciting its services are, but how well people care for each other when no one is watching.
I believe my calling is not to draw people to myself but to help them become more fully who God made them to be.

level_1.question: When everyone else in a situation is panicking, I often find myself unexpectedly calm, trusting that it will work out.
I have stepped into something that looked impossible from the outside because I believed God was in it, and I would do it again.
I pray with specific boldness. I ask for concrete things and genuinely expect God to answer.
I have a track record of stepping out in trust without knowing how it would work out, and I can point to what happened.
When a situation looks completely hopeless and everyone around me has given up, I...

options.text: Feel a quiet certainty that God is not done yet, and I hold that for others when they cannot hold it themselves
Start praying with whoever is willing and do not stop until something breaks open
Sit with people in the hopelessness and just be present with them in it

level_2.question: What energizes me most is not having everything figured out. It is trusting God when I clearly do not.
I feel frustrated when fear is treated like wisdom. Sometimes the boldest move is the right one.
I find it meaningful when I can be the person who holds steady for someone else who is losing their grip on hope.
I am more afraid of playing it safe and missing what God is doing than of stepping out and getting it wrong.
I feel most alive when I am...

options.text: Standing on a promise with nothing visible to stand on yet, and trusting it anyway
Leading people forward despite the uncertainty, because I believe in where we are headed
Praying with someone who has lost hope and watching faith rise back up in them

level_3.question: I believe that doubt is not the enemy of faith. Comfort and complacency are.
I believe that God is not looking for people with all the answers. He is looking for people willing to trust Him anyway.
I think the most dangerous prayer a person can pray is 'God, I trust you.' Because He tends to test that.
I believe that the stories worth telling are almost always the ones where the outcome was not guaranteed.
I think God often does His best work in the moments when every logical option has run out.

level_1.question: I pay attention to whether people at an event feel at home or feel like they are on the outside looking in.
Making someone feel comfortable in my home or my space is something I do naturally, without thinking much about it.
I notice the person sitting alone at church, at a party, at work, and I make it my business to go talk to them.
I find myself wanting my home to feel like a refuge for people, not just a place I live.
When a new family shows up at church for the first time, I...

options.text: Go introduce myself, remember their names, and make sure someone follows up with them later
Think about how we could create a better system for welcoming and integrating new people
Invite them to a small group or gathering where they can connect with people who have similar life stages

level_2.question: Creating a space where someone feels at home is genuinely one of the most satisfying things I can do.
I feel bothered when someone is new to a group and no one makes a real effort to include them.
I care deeply that people leave a gathering at my home feeling more known than when they arrived.
I do not think hospitality requires a big house or a fancy dinner. It requires genuine attention, and that is what I give.
People most often come to me when...

options.text: They need to be around people who feel like family, even if they are not family
They need someone to sit with them quietly and not make it weird
They want to be around someone whose energy is just steady and warm

level_3.question: I believe that belonging is a basic human need, and helping people find it is a sacred responsibility.
I think loneliness is one of the most serious crises the church should be addressing, and I take that personally.
I believe that opening your table and your home is one of the most underrated acts of love in Scripture.
I believe that no one should ever leave a gathering where I was present still feeling invisible.
I think the front door of a church is less important than how it feels to walk through it. And that is something I can control.
